title: Universal Service Foundation Scope And Principles Semantic Contract
description: Coverage-complete semantic contract for the universal service foundation scope and principles capability. Semantic information captured as a USF coverage record via the coverage record and equivalence audit; no external source code is imported and the V2 implementation language is an open decision.
aiGuidance: Coverage-complete draft grounded in semantic information (USF coverage record only; no source import; language open). Proof is hermetic/stale lineage, not current readiness. Enrich detail and publish fresh proof before generation; respect the hexagonal constraint in ADR 0005.

lifecycle.description: Governing meta-capability that defines the foundation's posture (semantic-first, evidence-backed, hermetically provable, AI-safe) and the lifecycle every other capability must honour rather than a CRUD service of its own. Its operational lifecycle is the V2 extraction lifecycle: each capability deploys a V1-proven contract and must keep its semanticCompleteness lifecycle/readiness model unchanged through extraction, with promotion only after the foundation matrix, registry, and validation checks pass. It owns no tenant records and is classified as a provider-dependency foundation concern whose health is the integrity of the foundation asset graph.

stateModel.description: The state is the foundation asset graph itself: the capability inventory, the status vocabulary, V2 targets, completion actions, the path map, the proof inventory, the migration chain, package status, the service matrix, and validator findings. It holds no tenant data; its state is configuration- and evidence-shaped, so backup scope is configuration/evidence only and restore can never fabricate tenant records. State changes are governed by deliberate semantic decisions rather than runtime mutations.

permissions.description: As a shared platform-governance asset it owns no tenant-scoped records and exposes no tenant-mutating routes, so standard platform security controls apply rather than per-tenant authorization. Authority over changes flows through the USF constitutional and authority order (semantic definitions, ADRs, validators, proof, then source), not through end-user roles. Its effect on tenants is indirect: it constrains shared platform behaviour without owning or exposing tenant data.

contracts.description: The contract is normative rather than an HTTP surface (the V1 contract reference is n/a): it asserts the principles, authority order, and provability guarantees that bind every capability's interface and extraction. Configuration is read from the declared provider/config plane and missing or invalid configuration fails closed instead of inventing defaults. Conformant capabilities must preserve their proven lifecycle/readiness contract during extraction and keep mutation boundaries closed under failure.

validation.description: Validation enforces that the foundation's invariants hold across the corpus: proof level is not report status, readiness claims must not exceed observed evidence, hermetic proof must not be upgraded to live external proof, and production-shaped must not be treated as production-live. It validates the asset graph for semantic drift and configuration loss, and treats stale or unknown signals as not-pass. Inputs that would violate the authority order or naming rules are rejected at the governance boundary.

errorModel.description: Failures fail closed: provider or dependency failure returns a typed degraded/readiness failure, mutation boundaries stay closed, and successful provider state is never fabricated. The principal risk is semantic drift or configuration loss rather than tenant data loss. Repeated typed errors past threshold, blocked readiness, or a failing promotion proof/smoke check are alertable foundation conditions.

auditModel.description: Because it owns no tenant mutations its audit concern is the auditable governance record: changes to the foundation move through Git history, ADRs, and validator findings, which form the durable evidence trail. Readiness and audit signals remain visible even in degraded mode so the foundation's posture is always inspectable. Audit here is about preserving honest lineage of decisions, not row-level mutation logging.

readinessModel.description: Readiness is the aggregate honesty of the foundation: a capability is ready only when its V1-proven contract, migration lineage, and proof inventory are intact and the matrix/registry/validation checks pass. Readiness fails closed when evidence is missing, stale, or unknown, and degraded mode keeps read-only readiness and audit visible. Promotion is gated on re-running the foundation matrix, registry, and validation after any fix.

proof.description: Proof for this capability is the foundation matrix plus the registry and validation test that demonstrate the corpus is internally consistent and that each capability's claims are backed by evidence, not assertion. Historical demonstration is via that hermetic foundation tooling rather than live external runtime. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: No distinct UI capability at the contract level (coverage matrix ui=0); UI is modelled separately and remains a future AI-customised deliverable.
